title: How Ukraine Figured Out Trump World
gist: Former Ukrainian FM Dmytro Kuleba argues Kyiv's Trump strategy is to placate while 'quietly decoupling' by building indigenous long-range and drone capabilities; he warns the 2026 war will be decided in the air, Patriot interceptors are the key shortage, and the war ends only when one side's will breaks.

insights.claim: Ukraine's strategy toward Trump is to placate, make offers, and accept what he offers so long as he doesn't cross red lines, while quietly continuing a strategy of decoupling from Washington.
insights.speaker: Ravi Agrawal
insights.so_what: A playbook other US-dependent states may copy: manage the patron while reducing dependence.
insights.quote: to placate, make offers, accept what Trump offers to you, if he doesn’t cross your red lines, while quietly continuing the strategy of decoupling

insights.claim: Kuleba says the biggest challenge with Trump is not changing his position but sustaining a favorable one, since Russia will constantly work to trigger a U-turn.
insights.speaker: Ravi Agrawal
insights.so_what: Trump's support is inherently unstable—any Ukraine-recovery thesis must price in policy reversal risk.

insights.claim: 'Quiet decoupling' means keeping the US relationship and taking what you can while building your own capabilities, because indigenous capability strengthens your agency.
insights.speaker: Ravi Agrawal
insights.so_what: Strategic-autonomy logic driving Ukraine's defense-industrial buildout and reduced reliance on Western arms.

insights.claim: Kuleba argues the 2026 war and beyond will be decided in the air, not on the ground, so whoever is better equipped for aerial war gains the upper hand.
insights.speaker: Ravi Agrawal
insights.so_what: Reframes the war as an air/missile-defense race—central to interceptor and counter-air investment demand.

insights.claim: The Iran war consumed roughly a third of the US Patriot interceptor supply, hurting Ukraine because Washington can now simply say it has none rather than make a political decision to withhold them.
insights.speaker: Ravi Agrawal
insights.so_what: Global interceptor scarcity is a hard supply constraint—bullish for air-defense/missile producers.

insights.claim: Trump agreed to license Ukraine to produce Patriot interceptors, but Ukraine first requested this in December 2023 and, as with Germany and Japan, standing up production will take months to years.
insights.speaker: Ravi Agrawal
insights.so_what: The Patriot license is a multi-year, not near-term, fix—Ukraine stays exposed to ballistic missiles meanwhile.

insights.claim: During a recent Kyiv attack Ukraine fired zero interceptors against Russian ballistic missiles for lack of Patriots; Ukraine intercepts drones and cruise missiles well but is defenseless against ballistics.
insights.speaker: Ravi Agrawal
insights.so_what: Pinpoints the exact capability gap Russia is exploiting—the ballistic-missile defense shortfall.

insights.claim: Kuleba argues the real story is not Ukraine's drones but how it built an entire defense-industry ecosystem from scratch in three years; drones are merely the ecosystem's output.
insights.speaker: Ravi Agrawal
insights.so_what: Shifts the investable insight from products to ecosystem-building—relevant to Western defense-industrial policy.

insights.claim: Europe is making a mistake by partnering with or buying from individual Ukrainian companies instead of building its own defense-industry ecosystem with Ukraine's as an integral part.
insights.speaker: Ravi Agrawal
insights.so_what: Points to where European defense capital should flow—ecosystems, not one-off procurement.

insights.claim: Buyers chase Ukrainian solutions that already work, but those products become obsolete within a week, so funding must go to the solutions of tomorrow, not only today.
insights.speaker: Ravi Agrawal
insights.so_what: Warns against investing in current-generation drone tech that will be superseded almost immediately.

insights.claim: Ukraine now strikes up to 2,000 kilometers deep inside Russia using its own long-range capabilities, ending earlier fights over German Taurus and American ATACMS.
insights.speaker: Ravi Agrawal
insights.so_what: Demonstrates successful import-substitution in strike weapons—a template for capability decoupling.

insights.claim: Gulf countries and, unusually, the US military are now eager to learn from and buy Ukrainian air-defense and drone technology—something unimaginable two years ago.
insights.speaker: Ravi Agrawal
insights.so_what: Ukraine is becoming a defense-tech exporter—an emerging market for its startups and IP.

insights.claim: Kuleba rejects the 'turning point' framing, arguing turning points are defined only retrospectively (as with Stalingrad) and that Ukraine has merely stabilized Russian pressure while increasing its own.
insights.speaker: Ravi Agrawal
insights.so_what: Cautions against over-reading recent Ukrainian successes as decisive—the war remains open-ended.

insights.claim: Kuleba says Putin is living his 'Stalin moment,' doubling down as things fall apart, and will not collapse within Zelensky's 40-day plan.
insights.speaker: Ravi Agrawal
insights.so_what: Near-term cease-fire is unlikely—base case is continued active fighting through 2026.

insights.claim: Putin is waiting for winter to crush Ukraine's energy system and resilience, and for elections in France to break European support for Ukraine.
insights.speaker: Ravi Agrawal
insights.so_what: Flags winter energy attacks and European election cycles as the key downside catalysts for Ukraine.

insights.claim: Putin does not recognize European leaders as equals and wants to negotiate only with Trump and the US, so European mediation efforts will gain nothing.
insights.speaker: Ravi Agrawal
insights.so_what: Any peace process runs through Washington, not Europe—European diplomacy is a sideshow.

insights.claim: Kuleba believes the war ends not in a negotiated settlement but with the collapse of one side's will—either Russia ceasing as an imperial project or Ukraine ceasing as an independent European project.
insights.speaker: Ravi Agrawal
insights.so_what: A binary, existential framing implies low odds of a stable negotiated middle—tail risks dominate.
